coredata.dc:title: An inner filter effect-based near-infrared probe for the ultrasensitive detection of tetracyclines and quinolones
coredata.dc:creator: Qian, Sihua
Qiao, Li’na
Xu, Wenxiang
Jiang, Kai
Wang, Yuhui
Lin, Hengwei
coredata.dc:description: Antibiotics play important roles in the treatment and prevention of bacterial infections. However, the over-use of antibiotics can lead to a lot of adverse side-effects like nerve damage, anaphylaxis and drug tolerance. Herein, we report an inner filter effect (IFE)-based near-infrared (NIR) fluorescent probe for highly sensitive and selective detection of tetracyclines (TCs) and quinolones (QNs), of which being the most frequently applied antibiotics. NIR emissive carbon-dots (NIR-CDs) that displayed broad excitation range, stable and strong emission were selected as label-free fluorophores. In the presence of TCs or QNs, efficient IFE from CDs to the two types of antibiotics occurred because of the good spectrum overlap between the ultraviolet absorption of TCs/QNs and the excitation of NIR-CDs. As a result, fluorescence emission of the NIR-CDs was obviously quenched, and this builds the foundation of TCs and QNs quantification. The probe provides linear detection ranges, i.e. 1–80 nM and 0.01–0.2 μM for oxytetracycline (OTC, an example for TCs) and norfloxacin (NOR, an example for QNs) with detection limits of 0.5 nM and 6.3 nM, respectively. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on NIR fluorescent probe for antibiotics assay. Moreover, the probe has been demonstrated to be very robust, and was successfully adopted for the determination of OTC and NOR in milk samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Near-infrared
Inner filter effect
Fluorescent probe
Antibiotics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Antibiotics detection by the NIR probe
Principle of the NIR-CDs-based probe for TCs and QNs
Detection of antibiotics in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China
National Natural Science Foundation of China

Ningbo Municipal Bureau of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21607160 , 51872300 and U1832110 ), the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China ( LY16B050005 ), Ningbo Science and Technology Bureau ( 2016C50009 ), and the W. C. Wong Education Foundation ( rczx0800 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the NIR-CDs, inset: corresponding diameter distribution. (b) FT-IR spectrum of the NIR-CDs. (c) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the NIR-CDs in water. (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of the NIR-CDs under different excitation wavelengths.

Fluorescence responses of the NIR-CDs toward different concentrations of OTC (a) and NOR (c). Fitting curves between relative fluorescence decrease and the concentrations of OTC (b) and NOR (d), inset: corresponding linear relationships between fluorescence decrease and OTC and NOR. (NIR-CDs: 2 μg mL−1, excitation wavelength: 380 nm; emission wavelength: 680 nm; PB buffer: 10 mM, pH 7.4). The error bars in (b) and (d) represent three independent measurements.

Fluorescence emission decay curves of NIR-CDs (a), NIR-CDs with corresponding adding of OTC (b) and NOR (c) under excitation at 588 nm. The concentrations of NIR-CDs, OTC and NOR are 2 μg mL−1, 10 μM and 10 μM, respectively.
Relative fluorescence decrement of the probe (2 μg mL−1) in the presence of different inspected substances. TCs and QNs: 10 μM, other kinds of antibiotics and interfering substances: 100 μM.
Principle illustration of the NIR-CDs-based inner filter effect (IFE) probe for tetracyclines (TCs) and quinolones (QNs).
Comparisons on analytical performance between previous technologies and this probe.
Analytical results of the OTC and NOR detection in milk sample.

head.ce:title: An inner filter effect-based near-infrared probe for the ultrasensitive detection of tetracyclines and quinolones

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Graphene Technologies and Applications of Zhejiang Province, Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology & Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo 315201, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Graphene Technologies and Applications of Zhejiang Province, Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology & Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Antibiotics play important roles in the treatment and prevention of bacterial infections. However, the over-use of antibiotics can lead to a lot of adverse side-effects like nerve damage, anaphylaxis and drug tolerance. Herein, we report an inner filter effect (IFE)-based near-infrared (NIR) fluorescent probe for highly sensitive and selective detection of tetracyclines (TCs) and quinolones (QNs), of which being the most frequently applied antibiotics. NIR emissive carbon-dots (NIR-CDs) that displayed broad excitation range, stable and strong emission were selected as label-free fluorophores. In the presence of TCs or QNs, efficient IFE from CDs to the two types of antibiotics occurred because of the good spectrum overlap between the ultraviolet absorption of TCs/QNs and the excitation of NIR-CDs. As a result, fluorescence emission of the NIR-CDs was obviously quenched, and this builds the foundation of TCs and QNs quantification. The probe provides linear detection ranges, i.e. 1–80 nM and 0.01–0.2 μM for oxytetracycline (OTC, an example for TCs) and norfloxacin (NOR, an example for QNs) with detection limits of 0.5 nM and 6.3 nM, respectively. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on NIR fluorescent probe for antibiotics assay. Moreover, the probe has been demonstrated to be very robust, and was successfully adopted for the determination of OTC and NOR in milk samples.

An inner filter effect (IFE)-based near-infrared (NIR) fluorescent probe has been constructed, and designed for highly sensitive and selective detection of tetracyclines (TCs) and quinolones (QNs).
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are powerful medicines that fight bacterial infections, and play important roles in many fields such as human health, food safety and increased production of livestock. Unfortunately, the abuse of antibiotics in agriculture and livestock husbandry brings great harm to human through the food chain, i.e. people's long-term passive intake of these antibiotics residuals [1,2]. World health organization (WHO) has announced a maximum allowable level (MAL) of 0.1 mg L−1 for antibiotics in food [3,4]. Antibiotics residues with level over MAL in agriculture and animal products may cause serious side-effects, such as tolerance, hearing impairment and liver injury [5]. Therefore, it is significant and highly desirable to develop high sensitive technology for antibiotics residues assay.

Up to now, various analytical methods have been adopted for antibiotics detection, for instance, liquid/gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [6,7], electrochemical method [8,9], colorimetric assay [10,11] and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [12]. However, most of these assay technologies involve in some native drawbacks, e.g. low sensitivity, time-consuming sample pre-treatment procedures, and/or requirement of large apparatus. In contrast, fluorescent spectroscopy as a sensitive and noninvasive technology has attracted broad attention in the detection of antibiotics. To date, except organic dye, many inorganic phosphors with stable and strong emission properties e.g. semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs), gold nanoclusters, luminescent metal–organic frameworks and lanthanide doped upconversion phosphors (UCPs) are promising candidates for sensing, and also have been designed for the antibiotics assay [13–18]. Unfortunately, these fluorescent probes exhibit some disadvantages, such as poor water-solubility and serious photobleaching of organic dyes [19,20], heavy metal toxicity of SQDs [21], and relatively low luminescence quantum yield and overheating effect of UCPs [22]. Thus, it is still challenging and meaningful to seek new phosphor-based probe towards antibiotics.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) as a rising inorganic phosphor has been paid increasing attention due to their fascinating optical properties (e.g. stable emission and high quantum yield), low-cost, easy preparation, excellent biocompatibility and good chemical/physical stability [23–25]. By virtue of these distinct merits, CDs are promising candidates in chemo-/bio-sensing, bio-imaging and diagnosis [26–29]. More recently, CDs also have been adopted to build probes for antibiotics detection and discrimination based on the mechanisms of energy transfer and charge transfer [30–32]. Unfortunately, these probes showed poor sensitivity or low S/N ratio (signal to noise), and hardly to be applied in real sample because of the spectral interference arise from scattered light and auto-fluorescence of most blue/green emissive CDs under the ultraviolet excitation. Herein, a near-infrared (NIR)-CDs-based probe that built on the mechanism of inner filter effect (IFE) has been designed for the detection of tetracyclines (TCs) and quinolones (QNs), two types of the most widespreadly used antibiotics. In the NIR detection window, the auto-fluorescence and scattered light from matrix are greatly avoided, enabling very high sensitivity of the probe. Due to the unique ultraviolet absorption characters of TCs and QNs, this IFE-based probe also exhibits good specificity even high levels of other antibiotics presented. Furthermore, this probe is further demonstrated to be applied for TCs and QNs detection in real milk samples.

All chemicals from commercial sources are of analytical grade. Formamide, KCl, NaCl, Na2HPO4·12H2O, KH2PO4, MgSO4, NaOH, CaCl2, glucose (Glu), ascorbic acid (AA), bovine serum albumin (BSA), trypsin, pepsin, were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Citric acid, L-cysteine, glutathione (GSH), ethylene imine polymer (PEI, Mw=600) and antibiotics (i.e. tetracycline (TET), oxytetracycline (OTC), doxycycline (DOX), chlortetracycline hydrochloride (CTC), ciprofloxacin (CIP), norfloxacin (NOR), enrofloxacin (ENR), ofloxacin (OFL), amoxicillin (AMX), gentamycin sulfate (GEN), erythromycin (ERY), chloramphenicol (CHL) and sulfadiazine (SDI), total thirteen) were provided from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. Milk was obtained from the local supermarket. All reagents were used as received without any further purification. Deionized water was used throughout the whole experiment.

The pH measurements were performed using a PHS-3C pH meter. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) was performed on a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer through KBr pellet technique. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations were performed on a Tecnai F20 microscope. The fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured on a Hitachi F-4600 spectrophotometer and a PERSEE T10CS UV–Vis spectrophotometer, respectively. Absolute fluorescence quantum yield of NIR-CDs was tested using Fluoromax-4 measurement system (HORIBA, JobinYvon. Inc). The fluorescence lifetime was measured by a Fluorolog 3–11 (HORIBA JobinYvon. Inc).

NIR-CDs were prepared via a solvothermal method as described in our previous study with little modification [33]. In brief, glutathion (0.5 g) and PEI (Mw=600, 0.3 g) was firstly dissolved and mixed in 15 mL formamide, and then the solution was transferred into Teflon autoclave. After heating at 160 °C in oven for 4 h, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally. Then, the reaction mixture was diluted with deionized water and directly subjected to dialysis (MWCO 3500 Da) for one week to remove unreacted starting materials and small molecules. Thereafter, the dialyzed dispersion solution was filtered through a 0.22 µm Millipore membrane to separate large particles. Finally, a dark green product was obtained under vacuum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibiotics detection by the NIR probe
ce:section.ce:para: Oxytetracycline (OTC) and norfloxacin (NOR) were selected as typical testing examples for TCs and QNs, respectively. In brief, appropriate aliquot of OTC (0–1.4 μM) or NOR (0–10 μM) solution were added into the NIR-CDs (2 μg mL−1) dissolved in phosphate buffer (PB) (10 mM, pH 7.4). Subsequently, the mixtures were transferred into centrifuge tubes and shaken for 1 h at room temperature. Then, the fluorescence emission spectrum of the sample was recorded using a fluorescence spectrometer. To examine the specificity of the probe, some ions, small molecules and other kinds of antibiotics were added into the probe in place of OTC or NOR with the identical experimental conditions. The emission intensity at 680 nm (λex = 380 nm) was taken for quantitative analysis.

Milk samples were purchased from local super-market. First, milk was pre-treated following a routine procedure with some modifications [34,35]. Briefly, 1 mL milk containing different concentration of OTC (0.2 μM, 0.4 μM, 0.8 μM) and NOR (0.5 μM, 1 μM, 2 μM) were mixed with 4 mL acetonitrile, respectively. And then, the mixtures were sonicated for 15 min. Subsequently, the samples were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to remove the precipitation of denatured protein. The supernatant was collected and filtered through a 0.22 µm organic microfiltration membrane to remove protein residual. Finally, the treated milk samples were 10-fold diluted with phosphate buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4). An identical detection procedure as in buffer was followed for OTC and NOR. The concentrations of standard addition of OTC and NOR in milk were calculated through the calibration curve that obtained in buffer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of the NIR-CDs-based probe for TCs and QNs
ce:section.ce:para: The probe for TCs and QNs was constructed on the basis of IFE-induced fluorescence quenching of NIR-CDs, as shown in Scheme 1. Owing to the outstanding merits of NIR detection window (e.g. low auto-fluorescence and scattering light, and deep penetration depth), NIR emissive CDs with broad excitation range from ultraviolet to red region (see infra) was selected as a competitive phosphor candidate. Among all kinds of antibiotics, only TCs and QNs are colored and exhibit obvious absorption band in the ultraviolet range (250–400 nm), which match well with the excitation spectrum of NIR-CDs. Thus, in the presence of TCs and QNs, fluorescence of the NIR-CDs would be greatly depressed according to the mechanism of IFE. Due to the excellent optical properties of NIR-CDs, the probe shows very high sensitivity towards TCs and QNs. Moreover, the detection window in NIR makes this probe to be suitable for antibiotics detection in complicated matrix like milk samples.

To realize this design, NIR-CDs were first synthesized and characterized. As seen from the TEM image ( Fig. 1a), the obtained NIR-CDs show uniform morphologies and good monodispersity with an average size of ca. 4.0 nm. In the FT-IR spectrum of NIR-CDs (Fig. 1b), the broad absorption peak from 3000 to 3500 cm−1 belonged to O–H and N–H stretching vibrations, which attested that the surface of NIR-CDs is rich in hydroxyl, amine/amide groups. The peaks at 1157 and 1239 cm−1 are attributed to C–O and C–N stretching vibrations, respectively. The amide bond is clearly confirmed by the typical vibrations absorption of C˭O, C–N and N–H i.e. 1662, 1387 and 1531 cm−1. The peaks at 1600 cm−1 and 1000–1100 cm−1 are classified as the stretching vibrations of C=C, C=N and C=S bonds, respectively.31 Subsequently, the photophysical properties of NIR-CDs were studied by the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectrometer. In Fig. 1c, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of NIR-CDs shows three typical bands at 250 – 300 nm, 350 – 450 nm, and 550 – 750 nm, which can be assigned to the π → π * transition of the aromatic C=C bond, π → π * and n → π * transitions of the aromatic π system that contains the C=O, C=N/C=S bonds, respectively [36]. The NIR-CDs display excitation-independent properties with optimal excitation and emission wavelengths at ca. 420 nm and 680 nm, respectively (Fig. 1d). Importantly, the NIR-CDs also show wide excitation band range from 200 to 600 nm (Fig. S1, Supporting Information (SI)). The absolute quantum yield (QY) of the NIR-CDs was determined to be 17.1% under the optimal excitation wavelength (i.e. 420 nm), which is a very competitive value to many NIR fluorophores [37]. In addition, the photostability of NIR-CDs under different conditions were tested. As shown in Figs. S2, S3 and S4, SI), the NIR-CDs showed stabled emission under continuous irradiation of a xenon lamp (150 W) for 60 min, good tolerance at high ionic strength and long-term storage stability under ambient conditions. These excellent optical properties enable the NIR-CDs to be promising candidates for the construction of fluorescent probes.

In this work, we took OTC and NOR as representative analytes for TCs and QNs, respectively. First, the time courses of fluorescence quenching induced by OTC and NOR were severally measured and shown in Fig. S5 (in SI), which demonstrates that the fluorescence of NIR-CDs decreased to the maximal degree after 60 min reaction and thereafter remained stable. As shown in Fig. 2a and c, the fluorescence of the NIR-CDs gradually decreased along with the corresponding concentration increasing of OTC (0–1.4 μΜ) and NOR (0–10 μΜ). In Fig. 2b and d, two fitting curves between relative fluorescence decrease and the concentration of OTC and NOR were obtained. The fluorescence of NIR-CDs was greatly and linearly quenched at low concentration of OTC (1–80 nM, inset of Fig. 2b) and NOR (0.01–0.2 μM, inset of Fig. 2d), and reached to a platform with higher concentrations. Based on the linear fitting, limits of detection (LODs) for OTC and NOR were calculated to be 0.5 and 6.3 nM, respectively, according to the standard 3σ (signal to-noise) criteria [38]. As far as we know, these LODs are the highest sensitivity compared to previous reports [39–44]. The LODs meet the food safety standard of European Union (EU) for maximum residue limits (MRLs) for OTC and NOR in food [40]. Such high sensitivity is mainly attributed to the merits of NIR detection window (avoidance of auto-fluorescence and scattered light) and excellent optical characters of NIR-CDs (high quantum yield and good photostability).

To determine the mechanism of OTC and NOR-induced fluorescence quenching of NIR-CDs, UV–Vis absorption of OTC and NOR, fluorescence life measurement were subsequently performed. As shown in Fig. S5a and b (SI), OTC and NOR antibiotics show obvious ultraviolet absorption band (250–400 nm), which match well with the fluorescence excitation spectrum of the NIR-CDs (Fig. S1). Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetimes of the NIR-CDs in the absence and presence of OTC and NOR are measured and determined to be 4.64, 4.67, and 4.68 ns, respectively ( Fig. 3), which clearly verified that there is no energy transfer process between NIR-CDs and OTC or NOR. The results have demonstrated that the NIR-CDs fluorescence decrease was ascribed to the internal filtration effect-based steady quenching mechanism [45].

To verify the specificity of the NIR-CDs probe toward TCs and QNs, the influence of other types of antibiotics, proteins, small molecules and ions were investigated. Three analogues to OTC and NOR were also assessed, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4, these analogues also cause obvious fluorescence quenching, which is mainly attributed to their similar absorption spectra in ultraviolet region (Fig. S5). No obvious fluorescence alterations are observed even when the concentrations of other antibiotics are 10-fold higher than that of TCs and QNs (i.e. 10 μM). Furthermore, other inspected species e.g. some small molecules (GSH, Glucose, AA etc), metal ions (Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe3+ etc.) also cause only tiny fluorescence variation even at a concentration up to 0.1 mM. These results clearly demonstrate excellent specificity of the probe for the detection of TCs and QNs. A detailed comparison on analytical performances (e.g. LOD, selectivity, and linear range) between recently published technologies and this probe is offered is Table 1. As seen therein, the proposed probe exhibits quite competitive sensitivity. In addition, because of the unique ultraviolet absorption properties of TCs and QNs, this IFE-based NIR probe is considered to be very selective.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of antibiotics in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Thanks to the robustness of the probe performed in buffer, the proposed probe is attempted to be applied for the detection of TCs and QNs (i.e. OTC and NOR being taken as typical examples again) in real samples. The pre-treated milk samples were 10-fold diluted with PB buffer (please refer to the experimental section for the details of pre-treatment procedure of milk). Six individual milk samples were analyzed, and the results were listed in Table 2. As shown therein, OTC and NOR are not detected, implying the eligible adjunction of these two kinds of antibiotics in the inspected milks. Following, standard addition experiments were performed to validate the determination. The standard additions of OTC and NOR were first mixed with the milk sample, and then pre-treated with the same procedures as that performed on the above mentioned pure milks. After that, the mixtures were 10-fold diluted with buffer and subjected to the probe. As seen in Table 1, the recovery rates are from 92% to 105% with RSD from 2.8% to 6.8%, which are acceptable for quantitative assays in real samples. Note that the pre-treated steps show nearly no effects on the antibiotics detection. And the analytical results demonstrate that the probe is applicable for TCs and QNs detection in real samples, which is mainly is ascribed to the outstanding advantage of the NIR detection widow i.e. an obvious avoidance of spectral interference of the auto-fluorescence and scattered light arising from a complex assay medium.

In summary, a NIR-CDs-based label-free probe was constructed on the basis of the mechanism of IFE, and designed for highly sensitive and selective detection of TCs and QNs. Thanks to the excellent optical properties of NIR-CDs, the probe showed high sensitivity down to nM level. In addition, the probe exhibited specific response towards TCs and QNs due to their unique ultraviolet absorption characters compared with other kinds of antibiotics and common components in food. The probe finally was applied to monitor the levels of OTC and NOR in milk samples with satisfactory results obtained. However, two drawbacks of this NIR probe should be mentioned. One is that it was built on the fluorescence quenching (i.e. signal turn-off). The other is the hopeless to discriminate TCs and QNs. So, fluorescent probe based on this NIR-CDs with signal turn-on and better selectivity is highly desired in the future. Nonetheless, the NIR-CDs as a competitive phosphor with excellent biocompatibility would show great potential in biology-related fields such as bioimaging, diagnosis and therapy and such work is underway.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21607160, 51872300 and U1832110), the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LY16B050005), Ningbo Science and Technology Bureau (2016C50009), and the W. C. Wong Education Foundation (rczx0800).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.10.097.
